sentences.grammarNote:
**Me siento** is the reflexive verb **sentirse** (to feel a certain way) — me siento, te sientes, se siente.
**Fatal** = terrible, awful — a strong colloquial word. Compare: **me siento mal** (I feel bad), **me siento fatal** (I feel awful).

sentences.base_language: I woke up late and I'm stressed.
sentences.target_language: Me desperté tarde y estoy estresado.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Me desperté' is the preterite (past tense) of despertarse: me desperté (I woke up). The preterite -é ending marks the yo form of -ar verbs. It expresses a completed action: me desperté (I woke up), llegué (I arrived), hablé (I spoke).

sentences.base_language: At work, I made a mistake on a project.
sentences.target_language: En el trabajo, cometí un error en un proyecto.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cometí** = I made/committed — preterite of **cometer** (to commit, to make a mistake).
**Cometer un error** = to make a mistake (literally *to commit an error*) — note this is not **hacer un error**, which would sound unnatural.
**En el trabajo** = at work — **en** is used for location.

Temporary emotional states use estar: 'está enojado' (he is angry), 'estoy estresado' (I am stressed), 'están preocupados' (they are worried). Use ser for permanent character: 'es una persona seria' (he is a serious person).

**Me siento** + adjective — the reflexive **sentirse** takes adjectives directly: **me siento nervioso** (I feel nervous), **me siento agotado** (I feel exhausted).
**Nervioso** and **agotado** must agree in gender with the speaker — masculine here.
Alternatively: **estoy nervioso y agotado** — both **estar** and **sentirse** work for emotional/physical states.

sentences.base_language: After work, I call my best friend.
sentences.target_language: Después del trabajo, llamo a mi mejor amigo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Después de + noun**: **después del trabajo** = after work — **del** is the contraction of **de + el**.
The personal **a** before **mi mejor amigo** is required when a person is the direct object: **llamo a mi amigo** (I call my friend).
**Mejor** (best) comes before the noun here: **mi mejor amigo** (my best friend).

sentences.base_language: Talking to him makes me feel better.
sentences.target_language: Hablar con él me hace sentir mejor.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Hablar** (infinitive as subject) = Talking — infinitives can act as nouns/subjects in Spanish: **hablar es bueno** (talking is good).
**Me hace sentir** = makes me feel — **hacer + infinitive** = to make someone do something.
**Mejor** = better — comparative of **bien**: **bien → mejor**, **mal → peor**.

sentences.base_language: I take a walk in the park.
sentences.target_language: Doy un paseo por el parque.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Dar un paseo' = to take a walk (literally 'to give a walk'). Dar has an irregular yo form: doy (not 'do'). Other expressions with dar: dar las gracias (to say thank you), dar una vuelta (to take a stroll), dar miedo (to frighten).

sentences.base_language: Now I feel calmer and more optimistic.
sentences.target_language: Ahora me siento más tranquilo y optimista.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Más + adjective** = more + adjective (comparative): **más tranquilo** (calmer), **más optimista** (more optimistic).
**Me siento más tranquilo** — **sentirse** with a comparative: I feel calmer.
Note: Spanish uses **más tranquilo** rather than a single word like English *calmer* — comparatives always use **más**.
